theorems.title: Halving-algorithm mistake bound
theorems.section: 3. The nature of absolute mistake bounds
theorems.proofHtml: Since there are only two possible predictions, the learner will always be able to choose a prediction agreed to by at least half of the current list of consistent functions. Whenever a mistake occurs, those functions that agree with the prediction of the learner will be eliminated from the list of consistent functions; these functions constitute at least half of the list. Thus at each mistake the size of the list will be divided by at least two. Since we have assumed that the target function is in the initial class of functions, there will always be at least one consistent function. Thus the method can make at most \(\log_2|C|\) mistakes.

theorems.title: General optimal mistake upper bound
theorems.section: 3. The nature of absolute mistake bounds
theorems.proofHtml: The halving algorithm immediately gives us the following theorem.
theorems.proofNote: The paper gives the justification immediately before the statement rather than as a following proof.

theorems.title: Characterization of the optimal mistake bound
theorems.section: 3. The nature of absolute mistake bounds
theorems.statementHtml:
Let \(X\) be any instance space. Let \(SOA\) denote the standard optimal algorithm defined above, and let \(C\) be any finite class of functions with domain \(X\) and range \(\{0,1\}\). Then

\[\operatorname{opt}(C)=M_{\mathrm{SOA}}(C)=K(C).\]
theorems.proofHtml: If we set \(k=K(C)\) and \(i=1\) in Lemma 2 we get \(M_{\mathrm{SOA}}(C)\leq K(C)\). Lemma 1 states \(K(C)\leq\operatorname{opt}(C)\). From the definition of \(\operatorname{opt}(C)\) we have \(\operatorname{opt}(C)\leq M_{\mathrm{SOA}}(C)\). The theorem follows.
theorems.proofNote: The proof is completed after Lemmas 1 and 2; both component lemmas are included separately.

theorems.title: Complete mistake trees lower-bound opt
theorems.section: 3. The nature of absolute mistake bounds
theorems.proofHtml: This follows trivially from the definition if \(C\) is empty. Assume \(C\) is non-empty, and let \(k=K(C)\). Saying that \(\operatorname{opt}(C)\geq k\) is equivalent to saying that for any deterministic learning algorithm \(A\), there exists a function \(f\in C\) and a sequence of instances such that \(A\) makes at least \(k\) mistakes when presented with that sequence of instances. Given an algorithm \(A\), we will show how an adversary can choose a function and a sequence of instances such that \(A\) makes at least \(k\) mistakes. The adversary keeps track of a current mistake tree. Initially this is a complete \(k\)-mistake tree for \(C\). If \(k=0\), the lemma follows trivially. Otherwise, the first instance chosen by the adversary is the label of the root of the tree. Whatever the algorithm predicts, the adversary tells the algorithm that its prediction is wrong. This response of the adversary eliminates some functions as possible target functions. The remaining candidate functions are either the class \(\xi_0(C,x)\) or the class \(\xi_1(C,x)\), depending on the algorithm’s prediction and the adversary’s response to it. One of the two subtrees of the root of the adversary’s current mistake tree is a complete \(k-1\) mistake tree for the remaining candidate functions. The adversary sets its current mistake tree to that subtree. It chooses the next instance to be the label of the root of the new current tree. The adversary continues in this manner, forcing the algorithm to be wrong at each instance. After \(j\) mistakes, the adversary’s current tree is a complete \(k-j\) mistake tree for the remaining candidate target functions. As long as \(j<k\), the root of the current tree has two children corresponding to non-empty subclasses of \(C\); thus the adversary can choose a point (the label of the root) at which it can force the algorithm to make a mistake. When \(j=k\), \(k\) mistakes have been made, as desired. The target function chosen by the adversary can be any candidate remaining after the last mistake was made.

theorems.title: SOA residual mistake bound
theorems.section: 3. The nature of absolute mistake bounds
theorems.statementHtml: Let \(C\) be a finite non-empty target class. Suppose that \(SOA\) is run to learn some function in \(C\) and that the sequence of instances it receives is \(x_1,\ldots,x_t\). Consider the variable \(CONSIST\) maintained by \(SOA\). Let \(CONSIST_i\) denote the value of \(CONSIST\) at the start of trial \(i\). For any \(k\geq0\) and \(i\in\{1,\ldots,t\}\), if \(K(CONSIST_i)=k\), then \(SOA\) will make at most \(k\) mistakes during trials \(i,\ldots,t\).
theorems.proofHtml:
We prove this by induction on \(k\), taking \(k=0\) to be the base case. By the construction of \(SOA\), the target function will always be in \(CONSIST_i\). If \(K(CONSIST_i)=0\) then \(CONSIST_i\) can contain only the target function. (If there are two functions in \(CONSIST_i\), then any instance on which they differ is the label of the root of a complete 1-mistake tree for \(CONSIST_i\).) The definition \(K(\varnothing)=-1\) ensures that \(SOA\) will always respond correctly when \(CONSIST_i\) contains only the target function. This proves the base case of the induction.

Now we will prove the lemma for arbitrary \(k>0\), assuming that it holds for \(k-1\). If \(SOA\) makes no mistakes during trials \(i,\ldots,t-1\) then we are done. Otherwise, let \(j\) be the number of the first trial among trials \(i,\ldots,t-1\) at which \(SOA\) makes a mistake. If there are complete \(k\)-mistake trees for both \(\xi_0(CONSIST_j,x_j)\) and \(\xi_1(CONSIST_j,x_j)\), then we can combine them into a complete \(k+1\) mistake tree for \(CONSIST_j\); we add a root node labeled with \(x_j\). Since \(CONSIST_j\subseteq CONSIST_i\) it is easy to transform this into a complete \(k+1\)-mistake tree for \(CONSIST_i\). But we have assumed that there does not exist a complete \(k+1\)-mistake tree for \(CONSIST_i\). Thus at least one of \(K(\xi_0(CONSIST_j,x_j))\) and \(K(\xi_1(CONSIST_j,x_j))\) must be less than \(k\). Since the response of \(SOA\) corresponded to the larger of these two values for \(K\), and since \(SOA\) was wrong, \(CONSIST_{j+1}\) will have the property that \(K(CONSIST_{j+1})<k\). By the induction hypothesis, \(SOA\) will make at most \(k-1\) mistakes during trials \(j+1,\ldots,t\). This gives the desired result.

theorems.title: Vapnik–Chervonenkis lower bound
theorems.section: 3. The nature of absolute mistake bounds
theorems.proofHtml: Let \(k=VCdim(C)\). Choose any set \(\{v_1,\ldots,v_k\}\subseteq X\) that is shattered by \(C\). Then we can construct a complete \(k\)-mistake tree for \(C\) with all internal nodes at depth \(j\) labeled with \(v_{j+1}\) for \(j=0,1,\ldots,k-1\). The nodes are chosen to be subclasses of \(C\) as required in the definition of a mistake tree. These subclasses will be all non-empty (as required by the definition) by virtue of the fact that \(\{v_1,\ldots,v_k\}\) is shattered by \(C\).

theorems.title: Mistake bounds imply equivalence-query bounds
theorems.statementHtml: The number of queries needed by the derived algorithm to exactly identify target function \(f\) is bounded by \(M_A(f)+1\).
theorems.proofHtml: Since every query after the first results from a mistake of \(A\), we have the following theorem.
theorems.proofNote: The paper gives the justification immediately before the statement rather than as a following proof.

theorems.title: Equivalence-query bounds imply mistake bounds
theorems.statementHtml: For any target function \(f\in C\), the number of mistakes made by the derived algorithm in learning \(f\) is bounded by the number of queries needed by algorithm \(A\) to exactly identify \(f\).
theorems.proofNote: The paper gives this result as the immediate consequence of Algorithm transformation 2 and supplies no separate proof paragraph.

theorems.title: WINNOW1 mistake bound for monotone disjunctions
theorems.statementHtml: Suppose that the target function is a \(k\)-literal monotone disjunction given by \(f(x_1,\ldots,x_n)=x_{i_1}\vee\cdots\vee x_{i_k}\). If WINNOW1 is run with \(\alpha>1\) and \(\theta\geq1/\alpha\), then for any sequence of instances the total number of mistakes will be bounded by \(\alpha k(\log_\alpha\theta+1)+n/\theta\).
theorems.proofHtml:
The total number of mistakes made during a run of the algorithm is equal to the number of promotion steps, \(u\), plus the number of elimination steps, \(v\). We bound \(u\) using the last two lemmas and then use the first lemma to bound \(v\). Combining lemmas 4 and 5 we see that

\[u/k\leq\log_\alpha w_i\leq\log_\alpha\theta+1,\]

or

\[u\leq k(\log_\alpha\theta+1).\]

Lemma 3 now gives

\[v\leq\frac{n}{\theta}+(\alpha-1)k(\log_\alpha\theta+1).\]

Adding the bounds on \(u\) and \(v\) leads to the desired bound on the total number of mistakes.
theorems.proofNote: The proof is completed after Lemmas 3–5; all three component lemmas are included separately.

theorems.title: Elimination steps versus promotion steps
theorems.proofHtml:
Consider how the sum \(\sum_{i=1}^n w_i\) changes over time. Initially the sum is \(n\); promotion and elimination steps cause it to change. Each promotion steps increases this sum by at most \((\alpha-1)\theta\), since when a promotion step occurs we have \(\sum_{i:x_i=1}w_i\leq\theta\). Each elimination step decreases \(\sum_{i=1}^n w_i\) by at least \(\theta\). Since the sum is never negative we have

\[0\leq\sum_{i=1}^n w_i\leq n+\theta(\alpha-1)u-\theta v,\]

giving the desired result.

theorems.title: WINNOW1 weight upper bound
theorems.proofHtml: Since \(\theta\geq1/\alpha\), the weights are initially less than or equal to \(\alpha\theta\). For any \(j\), the value of \(w_j\) is only increased during a trial in which \(x_j=1\) and \(\sum_{i=1}^n w_ix_i\leq\theta\). These conditions can only occur together if \(w_j\leq\theta\) immediately prior to the promotion. Thus \(w_j\leq\alpha\theta\) after the promotion.

theorems.title: Promotion steps force a large relevant weight
theorems.statementHtml: After \(u\) promotion steps and an arbitrary number of elimination steps, there exists some \(i\) for which \(\log_\alpha w_i\geq u/k\).
theorems.proofHtml: Let \(R=\{i_1,\ldots,i_k\}\). We look at how the product \(\prod_{i\in R}w_i\) is changed by elimination and promotion steps. Note that \(f(x_1,\ldots,x_n)=0\) if and only if \(x_i=0\) for all \(i\in R\). Elimination steps occur only when \(f(x_1,\ldots,x_n)=0\); promotion steps occur only when \(f(x_1,\ldots,x_n)=1\). Thus \(\prod_{i\in R}w_i\) is unchanged by elimination steps and is increased by a factor of at least \(\alpha\) by each promotion step. Initially \(\prod_{i\in R}w_i=1\). Thus after \(u\) promotion steps \(\prod_{i\in R}w_i\geq\alpha^u\), giving \(\sum_{i\in R}\log_\alpha w_i\geq u\). Since \(|R|=k\), for some \(i\in R\) we have \(\log_\alpha w_i\geq u/k\), as desired.

theorems.title: Lower bound for learning monotone disjunctions
theorems.statementHtml: (lower bound) For \(1\leq k\leq n\), \(\operatorname{opt}(C_k)\geq\operatorname{opt}(\widetilde C_k)\geq k\lfloor\log_2(n/k)\rfloor\). For \(n>1\) we also have \(\operatorname{opt}(C_k)\geq\frac{k}{8}(1+\log_2(n/k))\).
theorems.proofHtml:
Since \(\widetilde C_k\subseteq C_k\), it is clear that \(\operatorname{opt}(C_k)\geq\operatorname{opt}(\widetilde C_k)\). By Theorem 4, any algorithm to learn a concept class will have a mistake bound at least equal to the Vapnik-Chervonenkis dimension of the concept class. In the following lemma we show that the Vapnik-Chervonenkis dimension of \(\widetilde C_k\) is bounded below by \(k\lfloor\log_2(n/k)\rfloor\). This gives the first part of the theorem. We will split the derivation of the second formula from the first into two cases, depending on whether or not \(k\leq n/2\). If \(k\leq n/2\) then \(\log_2(n/k)\geq1\). Thus

\[k\lfloor\log_2(n/k)\rfloor\geq\frac{k}{3}\bigl(2\lfloor\log_2(n/k)\rfloor+\log_2(n/k)-1\bigr)\geq\frac{k}{3}\bigl(2+\log_2(n/k)-1\bigr)\geq\frac{k}{8}\bigl(1+\log_2(n/k)\bigr)\]

as desired. If \(n\geq k>n/2\), then \(\operatorname{opt}(C_k)\geq\operatorname{opt}(\widetilde C_{\lfloor n/2\rfloor})\) since \(\widetilde C_{\lfloor n/2\rfloor}\subseteq C_k\). (Here we use the assumption that \(n>1\).) We have

\[\operatorname{opt}(\widetilde C_{\lfloor n/2\rfloor})\geq\lfloor n/2\rfloor\left\lfloor\log_2\frac{n}{\lfloor n/2\rfloor}\right\rfloor\geq\lfloor n/2\rfloor\lfloor\log_2 2\rfloor=\lfloor n/2\rfloor\geq\frac n2-\frac12.\]

We also have

\[\frac{k}{8}\bigl(1+\log_2(n/k)\bigr)\leq\frac n8(1+\log_2 2)=\frac n4.\]

For \(n\geq2\), this is less than or equal to \(n/2-1/2\), giving the desired result.

theorems.title: VC dimension of monotone DNF classes
theorems.statementHtml: For \(1\leq k\leq n\) and \(1\leq l\leq\binom nk\), let \(C\) be the class of functions expressible as \(l\)-term monotone \(k\)-DNF formulas and let \(m\) be any integer, \(k\leq m\leq n\) such that \(\binom mk\geq l\). Then \(VCdim(C)\geq kl\lfloor\log_2(n/m)\rfloor\).
theorems.proofHtml:
Let \(r=\lfloor\log_2(n/m)\rfloor\). If \(r=0\), then the theorem follows trivially, since \(C\) is non-empty. Assume \(r>0\). Let \(s=2^r\). Note that \(ms\leq n\). We will construct a set \(S\subseteq\{0,1\}^n\) containing \(klr\) points that is shattered by \(C\). To describe the construction we will need to refer to an enumeration of the \(\binom mk\) ways to choose \(k\) distinct integers from the set \(\{1,\ldots,m\}\). Let \(\{(\kappa_{j1},\ldots,\kappa_{jk})\}\), where \(j\) runs from 1 through \(\binom mk\), be such an enumeration. (The values of the \(\kappa_{ji}\) are the chosen integers.) We will construct \(S\) as the union of sets \(S_{ji}\) for \(i=1,\ldots,k\) and \(j=1,\ldots,l\). Each set \(S_{ji}\) contains \(r\) points and each point has \(n\) coordinates. Split these coordinates into groups so that there are \(m\) disjoint groups of \(s\) coordinates each. There may be some coordinates left over; we will not make them a part of any group. Number the groups from 1 through \(m\). Fix attention on some \(i\) and \(j\). Let all coordinates of each point in \(S_{ji}\) be 0 except for coordinates in the groups numbered \(\kappa_{j1}\) through \(\kappa_{jk}\). Let the coordinates in groups \(\kappa_{j1}\) through \(\kappa_{jk}\) be 1 except for those in group \(\kappa_{ji}\). The coordinates in group \(\kappa_{ji}\) are used to distinguish the points within set \(S_{ji}\). Set the coordinates in this group to 0 or 1 in such a manner that for each subset \(V\subseteq S_{ji}\), there is a corresponding coordinate in group \(\kappa_{ji}\) that is 1 at points in \(V\) and 0 at points in \(S_{ji}-V\). This is possible since there are \(2^r\) subsets of \(S_{ji}\) and there are \(2^r\) coordinates in the group. For example, suppose \(n=24\), \(k=2\), and \(l=3\). If we take \(m=3\), then we get \(r=3\). Picking \((1,2),(1,3),(2,3)\) as the enumeration of the three ways to choose two integers from \(\{1,2,3\}\), we can take the sets \(S_{ji}\) as shown in Table 3.

Now we show how to construct an \(l\)-term \(k\)-DNF formula that is 1 exactly on some arbitrary subset \(U\subseteq S\). Each of the \(l\) terms of this formula will have length exactly \(k\), and no literal will be negated. Let \(U_{ji}=U\cap S_{ji}\). We will express the formula in terms of \(n\) variables, with one variable corresponding to each coordinate. This gives \(m\) groups of variables, corresponding to the \(m\) groups of coordinates. The \(j\)th term will contain one variable from each of the groups \(\kappa_{j1},\ldots,\kappa_{jk}\). We choose the \(i\)th variable in the \(j\)th term from group \(\kappa_{ji}\) so that it is 1 at all of the points in \(U_{ji}\) and 0 at points in \(S_{ji}-U_{ji}\). This is possible due to the way the sets \(S_{ji}\) were constructed.

To see that this formula is 1 on \(U\) and 0 on \(S-U\), consider any point \(x\in S\). This point will be in \(S_{ji}\) for some \(i\) and \(j\). The coordinates of the point will be 0 except in groups \(\kappa_{j1},\ldots,\kappa_{jk}\). Thus every term but the \(j\)th will contain at least one variable that is 0 at \(x\). Therefore the formula will be 1 if and only if the \(j\)th term is 1. The coordinates of \(x\) will be 1 in groups \(\kappa_{j1},\ldots,\kappa_{jk}\), except possibly in group \(\kappa_{ji}\). Hence all variables in the \(j\)th term will be 1, except possibly for the \(i\)th variable. Therefore the value of the formula at \(x\) will match the value of the \(i\)th variable of the \(j\)th term. This variable will be 1 if \(x\in U_{ji}\) and 0 if \(x\in S_{ji}-U_{ji}\), as desired.
theorems.proofNote: Table 3 is an illustrative instantiation of the construction; the proof text’s reference to it is retained, while the paper’s large binary table is not duplicated in the reader.

theorems.title: WINNOW2 mistake bound
theorems.statementHtml:
For \(0<\delta\leq1\), if the target function is in the class \(F(X,\delta)\) for \(X\subseteq\{0,1\}^n\), if \(\mu_1,\ldots,\mu_n\) have been chosen so that the target function satisfies the inequalities (1) and (2), and if Algorithm 4 is run with \(\alpha=1+\delta/2\) and \(\theta\geq1\) and the algorithm receives instances from \(X\), then the number of mistakes will be bounded by

\[\frac{8}{\delta^2}\frac n\theta+\left(\frac5\delta+\frac{14\ln\theta}{\delta^2}\right)\sum_{i=1}^n\mu_i.\]
theorems.proofHtml:
From lemmas 8 and 9 we get

\[\frac{u-(1-\delta)v}{\sum_{i=1}^n\mu_i}\log\alpha\leq\log\alpha+\log\theta.\]

Since \(\alpha>1\) and the \(\mu_i\) are non-negative, we can rewrite this inequality as

\[u-(1-\delta)v\leq\left(1+\frac{\log\theta}{\log\alpha}\right)\sum_{i=1}^n\mu_i.\]

A second inequality involving \(u-(1-\delta)v\) results from using Lemma 7 to eliminate \(v\) from the expression. This gives us

\[u-(1-\delta)v\geq u-(1-\delta)\left(\frac{\alpha}{\alpha-1}\frac n\theta+\alpha u\right),\]

and using the value for \(\alpha\) given in the theorem, we get

\[u-(1-\delta)v\geq u-(1-\delta)\frac{2+\delta}{\delta}\frac n\theta-(1-\delta)(1+\delta/2)u=\left(\frac\delta2+\frac{\delta^2}{2}\right)u-\frac{(1-\delta)(2+\delta)}{\delta}\frac n\theta.\]

Combining the two inequalities involving \(u-(1-\delta)v\), we get

\[\left(\frac\delta2+\frac{\delta^2}{2}\right)u-\frac{(1-\delta)(2+\delta)}{\delta}\frac n\theta\leq\left(1+\frac{\log\theta}{\log(1+\delta/2)}\right)\sum_{i=1}^n\mu_i,\]

and therefore

\[\frac\delta2u\leq\frac{(1-\delta)(2+\delta)}{\delta}\frac n\theta+\left(1+\frac{\log\theta}{\log(1+\delta/2)}\right)\sum_{i=1}^n\mu_i.\]

From Taylor’s formula with remainder, we get

\[\ln(1+\delta/2)\geq\frac\delta2-\frac{(\delta/2)^2}{2}=\frac\delta2(1-\delta/4),\]

and since \(\delta\leq1\) we get

\[\ln(1+\delta/2)\geq3\delta/8.\]

Thus, since we have assumed that \(\theta\geq1\),

\[\frac\delta2u\leq\frac{(1-\delta)(2+\delta)}{\delta}\frac n\theta+\left(1+\frac{\ln\theta}{3\delta/8}\right)\sum_{i=1}^n\mu_i.\]

From Lemma 7, we have a bound on the total number of mistakes:

\[u+v\leq\frac{\alpha}{\alpha-1}\frac n\theta+(\alpha+1)u.\]

Thus

\[\begin{aligned}u+v&\leq\frac{2+\delta}{\delta}\frac n\theta+\frac{4+\delta}{2}\frac2\delta\left[\frac{(1-\delta)(2+\delta)}{\delta}\frac n\theta+\left(1+\frac{\ln\theta}{3\delta/8}\right)\sum_{i=1}^n\mu_i\right]\\&=\left(\frac{(2+\delta)\delta+(4+\delta)(1-\delta)(2+\delta)}{\delta^2}\right)\frac n\theta+\frac{4+\delta}{\delta}\left(1+\frac{8\ln\theta}{3\delta}\right)\sum_{i=1}^n\mu_i.\end{aligned}\]

Using \(0<\delta\leq1\), we can simplify the upper bound to get

\[u+v\leq\frac8{\delta^2}\frac n\theta+\left(\frac5\delta+\frac{14\ln\theta}{\delta^2}\right)\sum_{i=1}^n\mu_i,\]

as desired.
theorems.proofNote: The proof is completed after Lemmas 7–9; all three component lemmas are included separately.

theorems.title: Demotion steps versus promotion steps
theorems.proofHtml:
We will examine how the weights are changed by promotion and demotion steps. We will use \(w_{i,\mathrm{bef}}\) to denote weights at the beginning of a trial in which a promotion or demotion occurs, and \(w_{i,\mathrm{aft}}\) to denote the weights resulting from the promotion or demotion. For a promotion step, we can write the update rule as

\[w_{i,\mathrm{aft}}=w_{i,\mathrm{bef}}+(\alpha-1)x_iw_{i,\mathrm{bef}}\qquad\text{for }i=1,\ldots,n.\]

Since a promotion step only occurs when \(\sum_{i=1}^n w_{i,\mathrm{bef}}x_i\leq\theta\), we have

\[\sum_{i=1}^n w_{i,\mathrm{aft}}\leq\sum_{i=1}^n w_{i,\mathrm{bef}}+(\alpha-1)\theta\]

for a promotion step. For a demotion step, we have

\[w_{i,\mathrm{aft}}=w_{i,\mathrm{bef}}-\left(1-\frac1\alpha\right)x_iw_{i,\mathrm{bef}}\qquad\text{for }i=1,\ldots,n.\]

A demotion step only occurs when \(\sum_{i=1}^n w_{i,\mathrm{bef}}x_i>\theta\). Thus

\[\sum_{i=1}^n w_{i,\mathrm{aft}}\leq\sum_{i=1}^n w_{i,\mathrm{bef}}-(1-1/\alpha)\theta.\]

Initially, the sum of the weights is \(n\); hence after \(u\) promotions and \(v\) demotions,

\[\sum_{i=1}^n w_i\leq n+u(\alpha-1)\theta-v(1-1/\alpha)\theta.\]

Since the weights are never negative, we must have \(n+u(\alpha-1)\theta-v(1-1/\alpha)\theta\geq0\). Thus \(v(1-1/\alpha)\leq n/\theta+u(\alpha-1)\), giving \(v\leq\frac{\alpha}{\alpha-1}\frac n\theta+\alpha u\), as desired.

theorems.title: WINNOW2 weight upper bound
theorems.proofHtml: Since \(\theta\geq1\) and \(\alpha>1\), the weights are initially less than or equal to \(\alpha\theta\). For any \(j\), the value of \(w_j\) is only increased during a trial in which \(x_j=1\) and \(\sum_{i=1}^n w_ix_i\leq\theta\). These conditions can only occur together if \(w_j\leq\theta\) immediately prior to the promotion. Thus \(w_j\leq\alpha\theta\) after the promotion.

theorems.title: Promotion–demotion potential bound
theorems.statementHtml:
After \(u\) promotion steps and \(v\) elimination steps, there exists some \(i\) for which

\[\log w_i\geq\frac{u-(1-\delta)v}{\sum_{i=1}^n\mu_i}\log\alpha.\]
theorems.proofHtml:
We will use the symbols \(w_{i,\mathrm{bef}}\) and \(w_{i,\mathrm{aft}}\) as in the proof of Lemma 7. This time we look at what happens to \(\sum_{i=1}^n\mu_i\log w_i\). We can write the promotion update rule as

\[w_{i,\mathrm{aft}}=\alpha^{x_i}w_{i,\mathrm{bef}}.\]

Taking the logarithm and multiplying by \(\mu_i\), we get

\[\mu_i\log w_{i,\mathrm{aft}}=\mu_i\log w_{i,\mathrm{bef}}+\mu_ix_i\log\alpha.\]

A promotion step only occurs when \(\sum_{i=1}^n\mu_ix_i\geq1\). Thus, at a promotion step we have

\[\sum_{i=1}^n\mu_i\log w_{i,\mathrm{aft}}\geq\sum_{i=1}^n\mu_i\log w_{i,\mathrm{bef}}+\log\alpha.\]

At a demotion step we have

\[w_{i,\mathrm{aft}}=\alpha^{-x_i}w_{i,\mathrm{bef}}.\]

Thus

\[\mu_i\log w_{i,\mathrm{aft}}=\mu_i\log w_{i,\mathrm{bef}}-\mu_ix_i\log\alpha.\]

For a demotion step to occur, we must have \(\sum_{i=1}^n\mu_ix_i\leq1-\delta\). Thus, at a demotion step we have

\[\sum_{i=1}^n\mu_i\log w_{i,\mathrm{aft}}\geq\sum_{i=1}^n\mu_i\log w_{i,\mathrm{bef}}-(1-\delta)\log\alpha.\]

Initially, \(\sum_{i=1}^n\mu_i\log w_i=0\). After \(u\) promotion steps and \(v\) demotion steps, we have

\[\sum_{i=1}^n\mu_i\log w_i\geq u\log\alpha-(1-\delta)v\log\alpha.\]

Since the \(\mu_i\) are non-negative, we get

\[\left(\max_{i=1,\ldots,n}\log w_i\right)\sum_{i=1}^n\mu_i\geq[u-(1-\delta)v]\log\alpha,\]

and dividing by \(\sum\mu_i\) gives the desired result.
theorems.proofNote: The statement says “elimination steps,” while the surrounding WINNOW2 discussion and the proof say “demotion steps”; this wording is preserved from the paper.

theorems.title: Mistake bounds under instance and prediction transformations
theorems.statementHtml: Suppose we are given transformation \(T_i:X_1\to X_2\), invertible transformation \(T_p:\{0,1\}\to\{0,1\}\), an original algorithm \(A\) that can accept instances from \(X_2\), and a derived algorithm \(B\) constructed from these as described above. Suppose that we wish algorithm \(B\) to learn a target function \(g:X_1\to\{0,1\}\). If \(f:X_2\to\{0,1\}\) is a function that can be learned by \(A\) with a bounded number of mistakes, and if \(T_p\circ f\circ T_i=g\), then algorithm \(B\) will learn \(g\) making at most \(M_A(f)\) mistakes.
theorems.proofHtml: Let \(y\) be the prediction that the derived algorithm \(B\) makes in response to some instance \(x\). For algorithm \(B\) to make this prediction, algorithm \(A\) must have made the prediction \(T_p^{-1}(y)\) in response to the instance \(T_i(x)\). We have \(T_p^{-1}(y)=f(T_i(x))\) if and only if \(y=g(x)\). Algorithm \(A\) is told that it has made a mistake when the derived algorithm makes a mistake. From the above we see that this happens exactly when the response of \(A\) to an instance \(T_i(x)\) is not equal to \(f(T_i(x))\). This can happen at most \(M_A(f)\) times.
